segments.s739-g17: هرآنکس که او داشت آزار و خشم is a relative clause: هرآنکس 'whoever' (or 'everyone whom'), که introduces the clause, او is the agent 'he', and داشت is the past imperfect/past auxiliary meaning 'had' (classical usage: 'he had [caused/possessed]'). آزار و خشم are direct objects (nouns) 'harm and anger.' The whole chunk means 'whoever he had harmed and angered.'
segments.s739-g18: یکایک به موبد نمودند چشم is the main clause referring back to those people: یکایک 'one by one' (each in turn), به + موبد marks direction/target 'toward the mobed (priest),' نمودند is the past plural verb 'they showed/turned (lit. 'made appear')' and چشم here denotes the 'gaze/look.' In classical Persian نمودن + چشم = 'to cast/turn one's eyes (upon)'.
segments.s739-g19: چو موبد چنان دید برپای خاست begins with the temporal conjunction چو 'when'; موبد is the subject; چنان دید 'saw thus/so' (chânan = 'that/so' referring to the foregoing); برپای خاست is a fixed phrase 'rose to his feet' (برپای = 'onto his feet/standing,' خاست = past 'arose'). Word order (temporal conj. → subject → verb → result) is typical of epic prose/poetry.
segments.s739-g20: به خسرو چنین گفت کای راد وراست: به خسرو 'to Khosrow/the king' marks the indirect object of گفت (he said to the king); چنین گفت 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus'); کای is a vocative particle 'O' (classical epic address), followed by the epithets راد و راست 'generous and upright' addressed to the king. The clause moves from reported action (he said) into direct vocative address.
segments.s739-g21: به‌روز جوانی شدی شهریار places the time adverbial first: به‌روز جوانی 'in the days/time of youth' (classical order), شدی is the second-person past 'you became,' and شهریار is the predicate noun 'king.' In older Persian the verb follows the temporal phrase and the predicate follows the verb.
segments.s739-g22: بسی نیک و بد دیدی از روزگار: بسی 'much/many' qualifies 'نیک و بد' (good and bad things), دیدی is 'you saw/experienced' (past), and از روزگار 'from/of the time/fate' indicates source or provenance. Together it states that the addressee has witnessed many good and bad events in the course of time/fate.
segments.s739-g23: شنیدی بسی نیک و بد در جهان is similar to the previous clause but uses شنیدی 'you have heard' (past perfective sense 'you have heard/learned of'), بسی 'much/many', نیک و بد 'good and bad [things]', and در جهان 'in the world' setting the domain of what was heard. The verb شنیدی governs what follows (ز/از ...).
segments.s739-g24: ز کار بزرگان و کار مهان: ز (poetic variant of از) marks source 'from/of,' کار here means 'deed/affair,' بزرگان 'the great ones' (magnates/elders), and مهان in epic usage refers to important persons (here 'nobles' or 'great men'); the repetition کار ... و کار ... emphasizes 'the deeds of the great and the deeds of the nobles.'
segments.s739-g57: بتشت here is the fused classical form of به تشت ('to/into the basin') — written without a space in the verse; ریختش is ریخت + the enclitic object -ش 'it' and functions as a past action 'poured it'; اندرون 'inside' marks location; خون گرم = 'warm blood' (خون modified by گرم). Word order is verb-object-location compressed in poetic style: 'into-the-basin inside poured-it warm-blood.'
segments.s739-g58: چو + نزدیک شد = a temporal clause 'when he/it drew near' (چو = 'when'); شد is the past/become auxiliary meaning 'became/drew'; تشت is the basin as subject/thing being handled; بنهاد is the classical past ('placed/put down'); نرم here qualifies the action adverbially: 'gently/softly'. The clause means 'when he/it came near, he placed the basin gently.'
segments.s739-g59: از آن تشت 'from that basin' sets the source; هرکس is an indefinite subject 'whoever/anyone'; بپیچید is the past/preterite of پیچیدن with the prefix بـ, 'turned' (here 'turned [his] face'); روی here means 'face' and is the object of پیچیدن in the sense 'turn one's face'. The clause describes people averting their faces from the basin.
segments.s739-g60: همه انجمن گشت = 'the whole assembly became' (گشت = became); پر before a noun means 'full (of)'; گفت و گوی is a compound nominal phrase 'talk and speech / conversation'. Together: 'the whole assembly became full of talk.' The verbless effect is typical of epic style: the state follows the participants.
segments.s739-g61: همی‌کرد is an archaic/perfective-continuous marker (همی‑ + کرد) giving an ongoing past sense 'was doing / kept on doing'; هر کس 'each person/everyone'; به خسرو نگاه = 'a look toward Khosrow' (به marks direction/target). The clause means 'each person continued to look at Khosrow.'
segments.s739-g62: همه انجمن 'the whole assembly' is the subject; خیره is a predicative adjective/participle 'staring, fixed [in astonishment]'; از بیم شاه 'from fear of the king' gives the reason (از = 'because of / from', بیم = 'fear', شاه = 'king'). The phrase = 'the whole assembly stood staring out of fear of the king.'
segments.s739-g63: به ایرانیان گفت = 'he said to the Iranians' (به marks the indirect object); کاین خون کیست is an embedded question 'this blood who-is?' where کیست (کی + ست) = 'who is (it)?' The clause reports him asking whose blood it was.
segments.s739-g64: نهاده is a past participle/past verb 'has placed/put' (classical بنهادن); بتشت here again stands for به تشت 'into the basin' (poetic fusion); اندر 'inside/in' reinforces location; از بهر چیست is an idiomatic question 'for what reason/for what purpose is it?' — از بهر = 'for the sake of / for', چیست = 'what is it (i.e. why)'. The whole clause asks who put it in the basin and why.
segments.s739-g9: فرستاد is a past 3rd-person singular verb 'sent/dispatched'. The following خسرو is the subject placed after the verb (classical/Persian flexible order): 'Khosrow sent'. مهان را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker marking 'the guests' as what is to be called; بخواند is a subjunctive/jussive or purpose-infinitive (3rd sg) meaning 'to call / that (someone) call' and expresses the purpose of the sending.
segments.s739-g10: بگاه is a temporal adverb meaning 'at that time / at the appointed moment'. گران is an adjective before the noun meaning 'weighty/important/precious'. مایگان is the plural noun modified by گران (here poetically 'the important/dignified ones' or 'magi/priests'); برنشاند is a past transitive (causative) verb 'he caused to sit / seated / set in place' with the understood subject (Khosrow).
segments.s739-g11: بدیشان is a prepositional pronoun 'to them'. چنین گفت is 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus', گفت = 'said' — in classical prose the noun-form ggf. stands for the speech act). کاین روز چند is an embedded interrogative/time-question introduced by ک(ه) 'that/that which' + این روز 'these days/this time' + چند 'how many/how long' asking the duration ('how long has it been').
segments.s739-g12: ندیدم is past 1st-person with negative prefix n- 'I did not see'. شما را uses را to mark 'you' as the definite object of ندیدم. The clause then continues with شدم مستمند — شدم is past 1st-person of شدن 'I became', and مستمند is the predicate adjective 'needy/poor', so together 'I became needy'. The two clauses are juxtaposed (I have not seen you; I became needy).
segments.s739-g13: بیازردم is a negative perfect/past-1sg compound meaning 'I did not hurt/annoy' (بی- prefix negates the verb). از بهر is a fixed prepositional phrase 'for the sake of / on account of'; آزارتان is 'your trouble/annoyance' with the possessive -تان. The whole phrase means 'I did not vex/annoy you for the sake of your distress' or more naturally 'I did not mean to hurt you (because of your trouble)'.
segments.s739-g14: پراندیشه is an adjectival/participial compound meaning 'full of thought / careworn / worried'. گشتم is past 1st/3rd-person form of گشتن used here as 'I became' (in context 1st person continues), and ز تیمارتان is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of your care/solicitousness' (ز = 'because of / from', تیمار = 'care', -تان = 'your'). Together: 'I became worried on account of your care.'
segments.s739-g15: همی‌گفت is the habitual/progressive past 'he kept saying / he was saying'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. پاسخ نداد is literally 'did not give an answer' (پاسخ = 'answer', نداد = past negated 'did not give'), and ایچ‌کس follows as an indefinite 'no one' (word order inversion is normal in Persian poetry/prose). The clause means 'and no one answered.'
segments.s739-g16: ز گفتن زبانها ببستند is a compact poetic phrasing: ز گفتن 'from speaking', زبانها 'their tongues/speeches', ببستند past 3rd-plural 'they closed/shut'. Literally 'they shut their tongues from speaking' = 'they fell silent'. بس at the end is an interjection 'enough' or 'that is enough', emphasizing the silence or termination of speech.
segments.s739-g89: همه مهتران خواندند آفرین is a short clause with subject (همه مهتران, “all the elders”) followed by the past plural verb خواندند (“they recited/sang/called”) and the quoted exclamation آفرین (“Bravo!/Afarin”). مهتران is an old/poetic plural for the leading elders/chiefs; خواندند here means ‘they uttered/chorused’ the exclamation rather than ‘read’ in a literal sense.
segments.s739-g90: که introduces a wishing/subordinate clause ('that/may it be that'); مبادا is an optative/negative-jussive particle meaning ‘may it not be’ or ‘let it not happen’. The phrase orders as [که][بی‌تاج وتختت][مبادا][زمین] where بی‌تاج وتختت is a compound describing ‘your crown and your throne’ (وتختت = and your throne) and زمین is the scope ‘the earth’ (= ‘on earth’). The whole yields a negative wish: ‘may the earth not be without your crown and throne.’
segments.s739-g91: بهی آن فزاید که تو به کنی is an optative clause (فزاید ‘may it increase’) headed by بهی آن ‘that excellence’ (بهی = good/excellence, آن = that) and followed by a relative-clause-like complement که تو به کنی ('which you effect'). کنی is the present/subjunctive form ‘you do/make’; the preposition به before کنی here is idiomatic/poetic (‘by/through’ or part of the verbal expression) linking the action to the excellence.
segments.s739-g92: مه آن شد بگیتی که تومه کنی is another optative/result clause: مه آن شد = ‘may that moon become’ (مه = moon, آن = that, شد = become), بگیتی = ‘in the world’. The following که introduces a clause describing how it comes to be (تومه کنی). The verb کنی is again the present/subjunctive ‘you make/do’; the sequence expresses a wish that the celestial image/‘moon’ come into being in the world by the addressee’s doing. (Note: تومه as supplied in the word list does not fit the immediate sense and appears to be a fused or corrupt form here; see senses.)
segments.s739-g93: که introduces a clause of result/intent. هم ... هم ... هم is the correlative construction ‘both ... and ... and ...’ listing participants: شاه (king), موبد (mōbed, Zoroastrian priest) and ردی (a lower/status term here used as 'the lowly' or 'the humblest' in context). The repeated هم gives the inclusive sense 'king and priest and even the lowly.' Note: in the provided word-list some tokens (وهم) appear fused; in the poem they function simply as the coordinating elements.
segments.s739-g94: مگر بر زمین سایه ایزدی: مگر here functions as ‘except/ save’ in older/poetic usage but in context carries the sense of ‘save/with only’ or ‘that there be upon’ — the phrase literally reads ‘save/upon the earth a divine shade’. بر زمین = 'on/upon the earth' (بر = on/upon, زمین = earth), سایه ایزدی = 'divine shade/shadow' (ایزدی = divine, سایه here understood also as protective shade). Together the segment expresses that all ranks may be under/receive the divine protection on earth.
segments.s739-g41: چنین گفت موبد is a fixed-order report: "چنین گفت" = "thus said" (literally "said thus") followed by the speaker noun موبد. The conjunction/marker که introduces the subordinate content clause. فردا پگاه are two time adverbials in sequence (فردا = "tomorrow" + پگاه = "dawn/early morning") giving the time of the proposed action.
segments.s739-g42: بیاییم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/hortative ("let us come" / "we should come") used to report a proposed action. یکسر is an adverb meaning "straight/all at once." بدین is the fusion of به + این ("to this") and functions as a prepositional phrase head; بارگاه is the concrete object "court/palace." The verb is implied to govern the whole phrase: "بیاییم ... بدین بارگاه."
segments.s739-g43: مگر here introduces a conditional negative sense "unless." The noun phrase پاسخ شاه (answer of the king) is the object of the verb یابیم. یابیم is a subjunctive/optative form ("we may find/obtain" or "let us get"), and باز placed after the verb means "again." Overall: "unless we obtain the king's answer again."
segments.s739-g44: که here begins an explanatory/circumstantial clause: "for/since." امروزمان has the possessive/associative suffix -مان ("our today" = "for us today"), شد is the past/coprusal verb meaning "became/happened," and سخنها دراز is the predicate "the speeches (sakhnhā) [are] long." Word order is Subject-Predicate with شد linking the time phrase to the predicate.
segments.s739-g45: دگر روز = "the next/other day" (دگر = "another/next" modifying روز). شبگیر is an adverbial/time expression meaning "toward night/dusk" or "at evening." برخاستند is a past, 3rd-person-plural perfective verb "they rose/rose up." The phrase places the action in time: "the next day at dusk they rose."
segments.s739-g46: همه is the subject/quantifier "all/everyone." بندگی را is the object with the definite/object marker را (here meaning their homage/service as a definite thing). بیاراستند is a past, 3rd-person-plural verb meaning "they made ready/arrayed/arranged," so the clause means "they all prepared (their) servitude/devotional service."
segments.s739-g47: یکی گفت = "one said / someone said." موبد ندانست is a short reported clause: موبد (the mobed) + ندانست (negative past of دانستن, "he did not know / he did not understand"). The final گفت repeats the reporting verb (a poetic device to mark successive speakers); here گفت functions as a coordination/report marker rather than introducing new content.
segments.s739-g48: دگر گفت = "another said." کان is an archaic/dialectal demonstrative (= "that/that one") introducing the reported clause. با خرد is a prepositional phrase "with wisdom" (با + خرد). بود is the past copula "was," and جفت is predicate "paired/matched/paired as a mate"—here figuratively "was paired with / matched by wisdom." The clause reports the opposing opinion.
segments.s739-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate temporal clause. آگاهی (ˈinformation/news) is the subject of آمد (past 'came'); ز + خسرو marks the source 'from Khosrow'; به راه is a locative adjunct 'on the road'. Word order is subject–verb–source–place (typical in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s739-g2: به نزد = 'to the presence of / to' (the prepositional head before recipients). بزرگان and سپاه are the two recipients, linked by و. نزد here means 'in the presence of' or 'before' (i.e. reported to the nobles and the army).
segments.s739-g3: که here introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. شیرین is the proper name (the beloved). به مشکوی is an adverbial/ descriptive phrase 'musk‑scented' (به + adjective-form), and خسرو شدست is an archaic perfective/presentive form meaning 'has become (Khosrow's)' — here implying Shirin has come into Khosrow's possession/relationship. The verbless possessive sense is typical in poetry: subject + adjective/adverbial + predicate 'شدست'.
segments.s739-g4: کهن بود = 'had been old' (کهن 'ancient/old', بود used in the past sense). کار جهان = 'the affairs of the world'. نوشدست here is a compound/poetic form (likely نو شدست 'was made new' or an uncertain/dialectal form); syntactically it functions as the verb phrase governing کار جهان, giving a contrast: what had been old was renewed. The clause thus states a consequence/result about the world's affairs.
segments.s739-g5: همه شهر 'the whole city' (subject). زان کار 'because of that affair' — زان = 'from/for that', کار = 'affair/event'. غمگین شدند is the past plural 'became sorrowful' (شدند = became). The particle order shows cause (زان کار) inserted before the predicate.
segments.s739-g6: پر here is the adjective 'full (of)'. The coordinated list 'اندیشه و درد و نفرین' names what they were full of: 'thought, pain/anguish, and curse(s)'. شدند repeats the past plural 'they became' and refers back to the people of the city.
segments.s739-g7: نرفتند is the negative past plural 'they did not go' (ن- prefix + رفتند). نزدیک خسرو = 'near Khosrow' (proximity object), and سه روز is a duration 'for three days' placed after the verb phrase, a common word order for duration after negative past verbs.
segments.s739-g8: چهارم = 'on the fourth (day)' (temporal adverbial). چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بفروخت is a classical/persian past form of فروختن with the prefix ب- (here used poetically) and can mean 'sold' or more broadly 'parted with/gave up'; in epic usage it is often metaphorical. گیتی فروز is an epithet 'world‑illuminating' (lit. 'giti' = world, 'froz/froz' = bright/illuminating) — probably a title applied to the ruler. Together this makes a temporal clause 'on the fourth, when the world‑illuminator parted/gave up ...' (the verb's precise referent or object is left elliptic/poetic).
segments.s739-g25: کنون (now) is an adverb placed at the start. تخمه مهتر is a compound noun phrase meaning 'the seed/lineage of the chief' where the ezāfe is omitted in poetic diction (takhmeh = lineage + mahtar = chief). آلوده شد is a predicative adjective (ālūde = 'tainted') with شد (past of شدن) forming 'became tainted' — شد is the past/copular verb taking the adjective as predicate.
segments.s739-g26: بزرگی is a noun 'greatness' used as the grammatical subject/topic here. ازین تخمه is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from this stock/line' (ازین = از + این, poetic contraction). پالوده شد uses پالوده (past participle/adjective 'purified') with شد to mean 'was purified' or 'became purified' — passive/result meaning; word order places the source phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s739-g27: پدر پاک و مادر بود بی‌هنر is a coordinated nominal phrase where the copula بود (past 'was') applies to the coordinated pair: 'the father (was) pure and the mother (was) unskilled.' پاک and بی‌هنر are predicative adjectives; poetic word order places the adjective immediately after the noun and the verb after the second coordinated noun.
segments.s739-g28: چنان دان is an injunction or instruction 'consider/know it so' where دان (here meaning 'consider/know') functions like an imperative/imperatival verb. که introduces a subordinate clause giving the content: پاکی نیاید ببر — پاکی ('purity/chastity') is the subject, نیاید is a negative jussive/present-form meaning 'should not/does not come' or 'should not be', and ببر here expresses the notion 'to take away' (an archaic/poetic form of بردن). The verb order is characteristic of classical verse: negative auxiliary + verb.
segments.s739-g29: ز کژی places the ablative/prepositional phrase 'from crookedness' first for emphasis (ز = poetic 'from'). نجوید is a 3rd-person negative/formal present ('does not seek' or 'will not seek'); در classical usage the negative is fused as ن‑ + verb. کسی is an indefinite pronoun placed after the verb (in negative context 'kasi' yields 'no one' / 'anyone' depending on negation). راستی is the object 'uprightness/straightness/truth.' Poetic word order and omission of an explicit ezāfe are typical.
segments.s739-g30: که introduces a reason/explanation ('for/that'). از راستی is instrumental/ablative 'by/from truth/uprightness.' برکنی is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'you/one uproot/remove' (from برکندن, here used in a general/second-person sense), and کاستی is the object 'shortcoming/defect.' The clause therefore states the consequence: 'for by uprightness you remove defect.'
segments.s739-g31: دل ما is the subject 'our heart.' غمی شد uses غمی (a noun 'grief') with شد ('became') to mean 'became sorrow/grief-stricken.' ز دیو سترگ is an ablative/cause phrase 'because of the demon's sight/gaze' (ز = 'from/because of'); سترگ (an older/poetic word) denotes 'sight' or 'gaze' and stands in genitive relation to دیو, with the ezāfe omitted by poetic license.
segments.s739-g32: که is a relative/subordinating particle 'who/which/that' linking to the previous clause. شد یار is a verb+predicate-noun construction 'became a companion' (شد = 'became', یار = 'companion/ally'). با شهریار بزرگ is a prepositional phrase 'with the great king' specifying with whom he became allied. Word order (verb before prepositional phrase) is typical of the epic/poetic style.
segments.s739-g81: بفرمان ز دوزخ is an adverbial prepositional phrase: بفرمان (بِ + فرمان) = 'by the command/order (of)'. The following ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' linking to دوزخ 'Hell'. توکردی is a past simple 2nd-person singular verbal form (تو + کردی) meaning 'you did/made', and بهشت is the direct object 'paradise'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: the instrumental phrase (بفرمان ز دوزخ) comes before the verb and object.
segments.s739-g82: همان here is an emphasizer 'exactly/that very' placed before the verb phrase. خوب کردی is a past tense clause 'you did well' (خوب = 'well', کردی = 'you did'); تو is the emphatic/contrastive pronoun repeated after the verb for stress. کردار زشت is the noun (کردار) modified by the adjective زشت and functions as the object/complaint ('an ugly/vile deed'). The phrase reads as an ironic appraisal: 'you did it well — [you did] an ugly deed.'
segments.s739-g83: چنین گفت خسرو is 'Thus said Khosrow' where چنین is a discourse adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (the senses list provided is for the noun 'conversation', so no matching verbal sense is listed — see senses mapping). که introduces the content clause 'that ...'; شیرین بشهر is a nominative phrase 'Shirin in the city' (بشهر = 'in the city/capital').
segments.s739-g84: چنان... که is a correlative/resultive construction 'so... that': چنان (so), بد (bad) and سپس که introduces the result clause. آن is a demonstrative 'that/the one', referring back to Shirin; بی‌منش is a substantive/adjective 'ill‑mannered/unprincipled (one)'. تشت زهر is a noun phrase where تشت 'basin' is specified by زهر 'poison' (i.e., 'a basin of poison' or 'the basin was poison'). Word order and omission of copulas are typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s739-g85: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'. تشت می‌شد uses the imperfective/past progressive auxiliary-like شد (می‌شد) meaning 'was becoming / began to be'. به مشکوی ما here is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'into/with our musk-scent' (مشکوی = 'musk-scented/of musk', ما = 'our/us'), so the clause reads 'the basin was becoming musk-scented by us/with our musk'.
segments.s739-g86: برین گونه is a fixed phrase meaning 'in this manner / thus' (بر + -ین forming برین, followed by گونه 'manner'). پربو شد is a past construction 'became full of scent' (پربو = 'scent‑laden', شد = 'became'). ازبوی ما literally gives the source 'from our scent' (از = 'from', بوی = 'scent', ما = 'our/us'), so the line restates the result: 'in this way it became fragrant because of our scent.'
segments.s739-g87: ز من is a causal phrase 'because of me / on my account'. گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned'; here it means 'became'. بدنام is the predicate adjective 'notorious/disgraced'. شیرین is the personal name 'Shirin' and نخست is the adverb 'first/initially' — together: 'Because of me Shirin first became notorious.'
segments.s739-g88: ز پرمایگان نامداری نجست is a compact clause: ز marks source/association 'from/among', پرمایگان = 'the great ones/nobles', نامداری = 'renown/fame'. نجست is the past form of جستن with the negative prefix ن- here yielding 'did not seek' (classical Persian uses ن‑ + past to negate); so the clause says 'she sought no renown among the nobles.'
segments.s739-g65: بدو گفت موبد is an inverted order: بدو (to him) + گفت (said) + موبد (the mobed), i.e. “the mobed said to him.” که introduces a reported subordinate clause: که خون پلید = “that the blood [was] foul,” with the adjective پلید following the noun خون as in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s739-g66: کزو (‘from which’ / ‘of which’) links this relative/ablative phrase back to خون. The main verb گشت (became) precedes the subject phrase هرکش بدید (‘whoever saw’), an instance of verb-before-subject inversion for emphasis: literally “became his-enemy whoever saw.” دشمنش contains the possessive -ش referring to the relevant person/thing in context.
segments.s739-g67: چو (when) + موبد + چنین گفت forms a temporal clause: “when the mobed said thus.” برداشتش is برداشت with the object clitic -ش (‘it’), here ‘he/they lifted/took it up’; the verb is past tense and the clitic attaches directly to the verb.
segments.s739-g68: همه دست (‘all [with] hands’ = everyone) is followed by بردست, an adverbial/locative phrase meaning ‘from hand to hand’ or ‘by the hand,’ and then the past plural verb بگذاشتند with object clitic -ش omitted in segmentation here (presented as بگذاشتش) meaning ‘they placed/put it.’ The phrase describes a collective action: everyone put it, passing it hand-to-hand.
segments.s739-g69: ز خون (lit. ‘from/with blood’) gives the material used. تشت is the direct object (the basin). پر مایه کردند is a resultative construction where کردند (‘they made/did’) takes a predicate phrase پر مایه (‘full / rich in substance’) — i.e. ‘they made the basin full (with blood).’ The final پاک functions as a resultative adjective: they made it pure/clean.
segments.s739-g70: ببستند is a past plural verb (‘they closed / they bound / they sealed’) and here heads a clause whose complement is the predicative adjective روشن (‘bright/clear’). The instruments introduced by به آب و به خاک (‘with/by water and by earth’) follow, marking the means by which they made it روشن/پاک. Order: verb + predicative adjective + instrumental phrases is common in poetic diction.
segments.s739-g71: چو (when) + روشن شد و پاک forms the temporal clause ‘when [it] became bright and clean.’ تشت پلید places the noun and its adjectival qualifier after the clause: ‘the foul basin.’ The verb روشن شد (‘became bright’) uses شد as the auxiliary/past of ‘become’ in classical usage.
segments.s739-g72: بکرد is a simple past (‘he/they did’). آنک introduces a relative/clarifying clause (‘that which / which’). او is the pronoun ‘he/it’ and شسته is the adjectival/past-participle ‘washed,’ so آنک او شسته = ‘that which he/it had washed.’ بد modifies the following element as ‘badly/ill.’ پرنبید is an older/poetic lexical item here functioning as the predicate of the relative clause; the whole clause reads ‘which he had washed badly.’
segments.s739-g33: The clause is a conditional protasis introduced by اگر (if). به ایران is a prepositional phrase ('in/into Iran') placed up front for emphasis; زن نبودی is a classical past counterfactual meaning 'there were not a woman' (نبودی is an archaic/past form used in such hypotheticals). جزین (جز این) attaches as an exception: 'except this one.'
segments.s739-g34: که is the relative/connecting particle 'whom/that' linking this clause to the previous conditional. خسرو is the subject (the king), بدو is the indirect object 'to her' (in classical Persian clitics appear before the verb), خواندی is a past tense verb 'called' (classical -دی past ending), and آفرین here is an acclamation/epithet ('Afarin' = 'praise'/'bravo') that he applied to her.
segments.s739-g35: نبودی begins another counterfactual ('had she not been'); چو here means 'as/like' comparing her to شیرین (the proper name Shirin). به مشکوی او is a descriptive genitive phrase 'in/with her musk‑scent' (مشکوی = 'musk‑scented'; او = 'she/her'). The clause as a whole describes her likeness to Shirin in the quality of musk‑scent.
segments.s739-g36: بهر جای ('for/to every place') is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'a place among ...' or 'a place for ...'; روشن is the adjective 'bright.' بدی here is ambiguous in the manuscript: in context it functions as a past action ('you would have given/placed') though the supplied senses list shows only the noun 'evil' — this is an instance where an archaic/poetic verb-form or dialectal variant is being used in the text. روی او = 'her face.' The whole segment says that (if not for the likeness) her face would have been given a conspicuous/bright place.
segments.s739-g37: نیاکانت is 'your ancestors' (possessive suffix -ت). آن دانشی is a nominative phrase 'that knowledge' (آن = 'that/the'), and راستان qualifies it as 'upright/righteous' (an adjectival epithet). The noun phrase describes a body of respectable/true knowledge attributed to those ancestors.
segments.s739-g38: نکردند is the past negative plural 'they did not do/make'; یاد is 'remembrance/mention' (object of نکردند); از چنین داستان = 'of/about such a tale.' Together: 'they did not make mention/recall such a story.' Note the verb-final preference and the object preceding the prepositional phrase.
segments.s739-g39: چوگشت is an archaic/composite temporal expression equivalent to 'when it happened/when it came about' (چو + گشت). آن سخنهای موبد دراز is a genitive chain 'those long words/speeches of the mobed' (سخنهای = 'speeches/words', موبد = 'mōbed, Zoroastrian priest', دراز = 'long'). The clause sets the temporal frame: 'when the mobed's long speech came.'
segments.s739-g40: شهنشاه is the subject 'the shahanshah/king.' پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply' and نداد is the past negative 'did not give.' ایچ‌باز appears as a vocative or epithet appended after the clause — here read as 'tippler/drunkard' (the term can be an insult or nickname) and its exact pragmatic role (address vs. description) is ambiguous in this line.
segments.s739-g49: سیوم (an ordinal: 'the third') is the subject placed before the reporting verb گفت (past 'said'). که is the complementizer introducing the content clause; امروز ('today') is a temporal adverb placed before the verb phrase. پاسخ دهد is a noun + present/optative-form of دادن ('to give an answer'); in classical Persian the present form here expresses the proposed action within the reported clause. This segment ends the reported clause’s verb phrase but the evaluative predicate (سزد) that judges that action comes in the next segment.
segments.s739-g50: سزد is an impersonal predicate 'it befits / it is proper' that comments on the prior clause; the following ز + و here function together as the clitic/dative ‘to him’ (classical spelling separates elements). که introduces the clause stating the intended effect: آواز فرخ نهد ('that Farrokh put forth/let out a voice'), where آواز (voice/call) is the object and نهد is the verb 'to put/produce' used idiomatically for emitting a sound. Note the classical/poetic ordering and the use of نهد (present form) to express the desired or potential action.
segments.s739-g51: همه ('all/everyone') quantifies موبدان (the priests); برگرفتند is a past plural verb 'they took up / took' formed with the prefix بر- plus گرفتند and governs the direct object راه ('the road'). Word order here is typical subject — verb — object, though راه appears after the verb as the thing taken up.
segments.s739-g52: خرامان is an adverbial/adjectival participle 'with a proud/stately gait' describing the manner; برفتند is the past plural 'they went/proceeded'. نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'near/toward the king' (نزدیک preceding شاه). The two clauses are coordinated in sense (they took the road and then, proudly, went toward the king).
segments.s739-g53: بزرگان (the elders/great ones) is the plural subject; گزیدند is the past plural 'they chose/selected' and takes جای ('place') as its object. نشست here functions as the following action 'sat' (took their seats); in classical poetic usage the verbal form نشست can appear after گزیدند to indicate the subsequent action, and singular form is used poetically for a plural subject.
segments.s739-g54: بیامد is the past 'he came' (here 'came in' or 'arrived'); یکی مرد is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain man / a man' (یکی = 'a certain/someone' modifying مرد). تشتی بدست is a frozen instrument/means phrase 'with a basin in hand' (تشتی 'basin', بدست 'in the hand'), describing what he carried as he entered.
segments.s739-g55: چو here is the temporal/simile conjunction 'when/as'. خورشید ('sun') + رخشنده ('radiant') form the image used for the subject's appearance; پالوده is a past-participle/adjective 'purified/cleaned', and گشت is the past 'became' (he/it became). Together this clause creates a simile: 'when (he) became radiant/purified like the sun.'
segments.s739-g56: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each in turn'. بران = بر + ان/آنان ('on/at those') and here functions as a locative/prepositional element meaning 'before/among those'; مهتران are the chiefs/elders (plural). برگذشت is a past verb 'passed by / moved past'. Word order places the adverb first for emphasis, then the locative phrase and the verb.
segments.s739-g73: This is a prepositional/locative phrase followed by its objects, but the main verb appears in the next segment. بمی (bami) is a locative/genitive form meaning 'at/over Bam'. بر is the preposition 'on/over' that marks the surface. پراگند is a past-participial/Verbal adjective 'scattered' that governs the following direct objects مشک وگلاب ('musk and rosewater'); in classical Persian the participle often appears before the auxiliary verb (here, شد in the next segment).
segments.s739-g74: شد is the past copular/auxiliary 'became' that completes the action begun in the previous segment. آن تشت is the subject 'that basin'; بی‌رنگ is the predicate adjective 'colorless/spotless' describing the basin; چون introduces a simile 'like/as' and آفتاب supplies the comparison ('the sun' or its radiance). The clause order (verb before subject in the previous segment) is typical of Ferdowsi's elevated/classical style.
segments.s739-g75: ز here (= 'because of, on account of') introduces the reason or relation; شیرین is the proper name 'Shirin'. بران is an archaic/directional particle (roughly 'in that regard' / 'thereon') that ties Shirin to the following noun. تشت is the noun 'basin' and بد رهنمون is a compound predicate 'bad guide' (بد qualifying رهنمون 'guide, omen'). The whole segment states that, in relation to Shirin, that basin served as a poor guide/omen.
segments.s739-g76: که introduces an explanatory or indirect clause ('for/that'). آغاز and فرجام are two parallel nouns 'beginning' and 'end'. Here چون functions as the classical interrogative/adverb 'how' (archaic; modern Persian would use چگونه), followed by بود 'was' (past). و links the two parallel questions: 'how was the beginning and how was the end.'
segments.s739-g77: به موبد is the dative phrase 'to the mobed' (indirect object). Such datives often precede the verb in classical Persian. چنین 'thus/so' introduces the speech; گفت is the past verb 'said' (here placed before the speaker's name, a common classical inversion), and خسرو is the subject 'Khosrow'. که begins the reported clause that follows (introducing the content about the basin).
segments.s739-g78: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/truly'. بد qualifies an implied noun (here the تشت mentioned previously) meaning 'bad'. این‌گر is an archaic conditional particle 'if this is so' (introducing the condition). دگرگونه is the adjective 'different/otherwise', and گشت is the past 'became/turned' used as the main verb. Together they express: 'Truly, if it is bad, it has become different/otherwise.'
segments.s739-g79: بدو is the short dative pronoun 'to him' (to that person). گفت موبد is inverted order for 'the mobed said' (classical word order often places the verb before the subject). که introduces the reported content. نوشه بدی is a genitive/attributive phrase: نوشه 'a draught/portion (or elixir)' qualified by بدی 'of evil' — 'an evil draught' or 'a harmful portion' — which the next clause explains.
segments.s739-g80: پدیدار is a noun 'appearance/manifestation' (used here as the subject of the clause). شد is the past 'became/appeared' forming the verbal predicate. نیکوی is the adjective 'good' (postpositive: 'a good/beneficial thing'), and از بدی is the source phrase 'from the bad/evil'. The clause thus literally says 'a good manifestation became from the bad' — i.e. 'a good thing appeared out of the evil.'

sentences.s739-s9: Everyone whom he had harmed and angered, one by one they turned their gaze to the mobed (priest).
sentences.s739-s10: When the mobed saw this, he rose to his feet and said to the king: 'O generous and upright one...'
sentences.s739-s11: In the days of your youth you became king; you have seen much good and ill from the course of time.
sentences.s739-s12: You have heard many good and bad things in the world about the deeds of the great and the deeds of the nobles.
sentences.s739-s29: He poured his warm blood into the basin; when he came near, he set the basin down gently.
sentences.s739-s30: From that basin, whoever turned his face away — the whole assembly became full of talk and conversation.
sentences.s739-s31: Each person kept looking at Khosrow; the whole assembly stared in fear of the king.
sentences.s739-s32: He said to the Iranians, 'Whose blood is this?' — 'Who has placed it in the basin and for what (purpose)?'
sentences.s739-s5: Khosrow sent (orders) to have the guests summoned, and at the appointed time he seated the important m… (the dignified ones).
sentences.s739-s6: He said to them thus: 'How long (these days)? I have not seen you; I have become needy.'
sentences.s739-s7: I did not offend/annoy you for the sake of your trouble; I became anxious and careworn because of your solicitousness.
sentences.s739-s8: He kept speaking, and no one answered; they closed their tongues from speaking — enough (they were silent).
sentences.s739-s45: All the elders cried “Afarin!” — may the earth never be without your crown and throne.
sentences.s739-s46: May that excellence increase which you bring about; may that moon be made in the world as you fashion it.
sentences.s739-s47: so that the king, the mobed, and even the lowly alike may have, upon the earth, the divine shade.
sentences.s739-s21: The mobed said thus: that tomorrow at dawn we should come straight to this court.
sentences.s739-s22: Unless we obtain the king's answer again, for today our speeches have become long.
sentences.s739-s23: The next day at dusk they rose up, and they all prepared their servitude.
sentences.s739-s24: One said, 'The mobed did not know,' another said, 'that one was matched with wisdom.'
sentences.s739-s1: When news came from Khosrow on the road to the nobles and to the army.
sentences.s739-s2: For Shirin, musk‑scented, has become Khosrow's; the world's affairs, which had been old, were made new.
sentences.s739-s3: The whole city was saddened by that affair; people were filled with thought, anguish, and curses.
sentences.s739-s4: They did not go near Khosrow for three days; on the fourth, when the world‑illuminator (giti‑frouz) had parted (i.e. given up/let go), ...
sentences.s739-s13: Now the seed/line of the chief became tainted; greatness from this line was purified.
sentences.s739-s14: The father was pure and the mother was unskilled; consider it so — that purity should not be taken away.
sentences.s739-s15: From crookedness no one seeks straightness, for by straightness you (one) will uproot defect.
sentences.s739-s16: Our heart became sorrowful because of the demon's (piercing) gaze, which became a companion to the great king.
sentences.s739-s41: By the command of Hell you made Paradise; truly you did well — you committed an ugly deed.
sentences.s739-s42: Thus Khosrow said that Shirin in the city was so bad that that ill‑mannered one’s basin was poison.
sentences.s739-s43: Now the basin began to take on our muskiness; in this manner it became full of our fragrance.
sentences.s739-s44: Because of me Shirin first became notorious; she sought no renown from the nobles.
sentences.s739-s33: The mobed told him that the blood was foul; whoever saw it became his enemy.
sentences.s739-s34: When the mobed thus spoke, he took it up, and everyone passed it from hand to hand.
sentences.s739-s35: They filled the basin with blood, made it full of substance, and purified it — they washed it bright with water and with earth.
sentences.s739-s36: When the basin became bright and clean, he did to the foul basin what he had washed badly.
sentences.s739-s17: If in Iran there were no woman but this one — whom Khosrow called “Afarin.”
sentences.s739-s18: Had she not been like Shirin, musk‑scented, you would have given her face a place among the bright.
sentences.s739-s19: Your ancestors, the upright in that knowledge, did not make mention of such a tale.
sentences.s739-s20: When the mobed's long speech ended, the shahanshah gave no answer, O tippler.
sentences.s739-s25: The third said that today it would be fitting to give him an answer, so that Farrokh might raise (let out) a voice.
sentences.s739-s26: All the mobeds took up the road; stately, they proceeded toward the king.
sentences.s739-s27: The elders chose places and sat; a man came in, carrying a basin in his hand.
sentences.s739-s28: When (his) face became radiant and cleansed like the sun, he passed by those chiefs one by one.
sentences.s739-s37: They scattered musk and rosewater over Bam; that basin became colorless and shone like the sun.
sentences.s739-s38: Because of Shirin, that basin was a poor guide — how its beginning was and how its end was.
sentences.s739-s39: Khosrow said to the mobed thus: 'Truly, if the basin is bad, it has turned out otherwise.'
sentences.s739-s40: The mobed told him that from the evil draught a good thing had appeared.
